mechanism: animation frame-event hashes: ToolX tags specific animation frames with a calcHash event id; on playback reaching that frame the engine sends NM_ANIMATION_START (msg param_2==1) with the hash as param_3; entity handler matches it to fire a sound/projectile/hitbox. Same calcHash namespace as assets (overlaps).
cross_validation: 0x146CE002 frame-event = our verified FX_KLAY_DIE_EXPLODE asset name. Proves frame-events share the calcHash namespace AND the functional naming vocabulary.
concentration: ~25 of 39 fire from PlayerCallback_8005e574 (one Klaymen state w/ many tagged frames) + clusters in 8005d0c8/cce0/d404. EntityCallback_80047768 owns 0x10D86282 + 0xB0C10420 (x4 each) = a boss/enemy with 2 frame events.
why_crackable: UNLIKE asset names (hashed at build, string discarded), a frame-event fires at a KNOWN, REPRODUCIBLE gameplay moment. The hash is observable at its trigger.
breakpoint_crack_strategy: 1. In an emulator (PCSX-Redux/no$), set a breakpoint on each param_3== comparison site (addresses known from the decomp line->fn map).
2. Play the moment that triggers it (e.g. let Klaymen die -> catches 0x146CE002; do a specific Klaymen action -> catches the 8005e574 events).
3. You SEE what happened on screen at the break -> you know the SEMANTIC (footstep, fire, hitbox-on, sound X).

4. That collapses the name to a tiny functional vocabulary (FOOTSTEP/FIRE/HIT/STEP/SHOOT/LAND/...) -> brute-force calcHash over that vocab almost certainly resolves it (short functional words, like FX_KLAY_DIE_EXPLODE).

5. Bonus: breakpoint the calcHash routine itself / the anim-frame dispatcher and read the event STRING from the sequence data if ToolX left it (it likely passes the hash, but the exposure-sheet tag MIGHT be present in sequence data loaded to RAM).
why_better_than_asset_cracking: Asset names: no observation point, lossy hash, dead end offline. Frame-events: live observation point + known semantic + short functional name = HIGH crack probability with an emulator. This is the single best remaining naming attack vector.

offline_crack_attempt.result: NO new names. Only 0x146CE002=FX_KLAY_DIE_EXPLODE (already known) hit a functional vocab.
offline_crack_attempt.why: popcount shows most event names are LONG (9-14 chars, pc 9-14), not short tags. Short ones (0x50900004 pc=5) are too ambiguous; long ones unguessable without semantic. Same wall as asset names.
offline_crack_attempt.family_structure: cluster 0x10062824/0x80062824/0x92062824/0x94062824/0x98062824/0xB0062824 share tail ..062824, differ only in top byte = ONE base event name + varying prefix char (a named family, like the sprite clusters). ~25 events fire from PlayerCallback_8005e574 = one Klaymen state.
offline_crack_attempt.implication: breakpoint ONE member of the ..062824 family in-emulator while doing that Klaymen action -> semantic for the WHOLE family at once -> collapses to tiny vocab. Confirms the breakpoint approach is the unlock, offline guessing is not.
